solution:
Given,

The coefficient of x9 in αx3+1βx11and the coefficient of x-9 in αx-1βx311 are equal,

Now the rth term of αx3+1βx11 is given by =Cr11·α11-rβr×x311-r-r

So, for x9, 33-4r=9⇒r=6

Hence, coefficient of x9 in αx3+1βx11=C611·α5β6

Similarly, for coefficient of x-9 in αx-1βx311=-C511·α6β5

Now given both coefficient are equal,

So, 11C6·α5β6=-11C5·α6β5

⇒1β=-α

⇒αβ=-1

⇒(αβ)2=1

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The expansion of $(1 + \alpha x)^{26}$ has $27$ terms. The middle term is $T_{14}$.

The coefficient of the middle term is $^{26}C_{13} \alpha^{13}$.

The expansion of $(1 - \alpha x)^{28}$ has $29$ terms. The middle term is $T_{15}$.

The coefficient of the middle term is $^{28}C_{14} (-\alpha)^{14} = ^{28}C_{14} \alpha^{14}$.

Equating the two coefficients:

$^{26}C_{13} \alpha^{13} = ^{28}C_{14} \alpha^{14}$

Since $\alpha \neq 0$, dividing by $\alpha^{13}$ gives:

$\alpha = \dfrac{^{26}C_{13}}{^{28}C_{14}}$

Expanding the combinations using factorials:

$^{28}C_{14} = \dfrac{28!}{14! 14!} = \dfrac{28 \times 27 \times 26!}{14 \times 13! \times 14 \times 13!} = \dfrac{28 \times 27}{14 \times 14} \times \dfrac{26!}{13! 13!}$

$^{28}C_{14} = \dfrac{27}{7} \times ^{26}C_{13}$

Substituting this back:

$\alpha = \dfrac{^{26}C_{13}}{\dfrac{27}{7} \times ^{26}C_{13}} = \dfrac{7}{27}$

Answer: $\dfrac{7}{27}$

So, when 643232 is divided by 9 we get 1 as remainder.

x=22, 2-2

Here, only x=14 is given in the options.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The general term of the product is $T_r = \frac{1}{^{15}C_{r-1}} + \frac{1}{^{15}C_r}$ for $r = 1, 2, \dots, 13$.
Using the formula $^{n}C_r = \frac{n}{r} \cdot ^{n-1}C_{r-1}$, we have $^{15}C_r = \frac{15}{r} \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1}$ and $^{15}C_{r-1} = \frac{15}{15-(r-1)} \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1} = \frac{15}{16-r} \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1}$.
Substituting these into $T_r$:
$T_r = \frac{16-r}{15 \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1}} + \frac{r}{15 \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1}} = \frac{16-r+r}{15 \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1}} = \frac{16}{15 \cdot ^{14}C_{r-1}}$.
The product is $\prod_{r=1}^{13} T_r= \prod_{r=1}^{13} \frac{16}{15\cdot {^{14}C_{r-1}}}= \frac{16^{13}}{15^{13} \cdot \prod_{r=1}^{13}{^{14}C_{r-1}}}$.
The denominator of the product is $15^{13} \cdot (^{14}C_0 \cdot ^{14}C_1 \dots ^{14}C_{12})$.
Comparing this with the given expression $\frac{\alpha^{13}}{^{14}C_0 \cdot ^{14}C_1 \dots ^{14}C_{12}}$, we get:
$\alpha^{13} = \frac{16^{13}}{15^{13}} \implies \alpha = \frac{16}{15}$.
We need to find the value of $30\alpha$:
$30\alpha = 30 \times \frac{16}{15} = 2 \times 16 = 32$.

solution:
We know the binomial expansion of a+bn is,

a+bn=C0nanb0+C1nan-1b1+C2nan-2b2+......+Cnna0bn

Using the above expansion,

x=83+1313=C0138313+C1138312131+…

Let x'=83-1313=C0138313-C1138312131+…

So, x-x'=2C113·8312131+C3138310·133…

Therefore, x-x' is an even integer, hence x is even

Now, y=72+99=C09729+C1972891+C2972792+....Now let,y'=(72-9)9=C09(72)9-C19(72)8(9)1+C2972792

So, y-y'=2C1972891+C3972693+…

y-y'= Even integer hence y is even

solution:
(1+x)n= nC0+ nC1x+ nC2x2+......+ nCnxn
Diff. w. r. t. x
⇒n(1+x)n-1= nC1 + nC2(2x)+.....+ nCnn(x)n-1
Multiply by x both side
⇒nx(1+x)n-1= nC1x+ nC22x2+.....+ nCnnxn
Diff. w. r. t. x
⇒n[(1+x)n-1+(n-1)x(1+x)n-2] = nC1 + nC222X+......+ nCn(n2)xn-1
Put x=1 and n=20
⇒ 20C1+ 22 20C2+  32 20C3+......+(20)2 20C20
=20×2182+19=420218=A2β
Hence, A,β=(420, 18)

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given expansion is $\left(y^{1 / 5}+x^{1 / 10}\right)^{55}$ The general term is
$$
T_{r+1}={ }^{55} \mathrm{C}_r\left(y^{1 / 5}\right)^{55-r} \cdot\left(x^{\frac{1}{10}}\right)^r
$$

$T_{r+1}$ would free from radical sign if powers of $y$ and $x$ are integers.
i.e. $\frac{55-r}{5}$ and $\frac{r}{10}$ are integer.
$\Rightarrow r$ is multiple of 10 .
Hence, $r=0,10,20,30,40,50$
It is an A.P.
Thus, $50=0+(k-1) 10$ $50=10 k-10 \Rightarrow k=6$
Thus, the six terms of the given expansion in which $x$ and $y$ are free from radical signs.

=7m1+m2+7m3+1-1

=7m1+m2+m3

This is a multiple of 7

Hence, remainder is 0

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}+3}{2^{\mathrm{r}}}\right) \cdot{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}=\alpha\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9-\beta, \alpha, \beta \in \mathrm{N}$
Now,
$\begin{aligned}
& \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}+3}{2^{\mathrm{r}}}\right) \cdot{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}=\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9\left(\frac{\mathrm{r}}{2^{\mathrm{r}}}\right) \cdot{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}+\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9\left(\frac{3}{2^{\mathrm{r}}}\right) \cdot{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}} \\
& =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9\left(\frac{9}{2^{\mathrm{r}}}\right) \cdot{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}-1}+3 \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{r}}\left[\mathrm{U} \sin \mathrm{~g} \frac{{ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}{{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}-1}}=\frac{9}{\mathrm{r}}\right] \\
& =\frac{9}{2} \sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^9{ }^8 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{r}-1}+3\left(\sum_{\mathrm{r}=0}^9\left({ }^9 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\mathrm{r}}\right)-1\right) \\
& =\frac{9}{2}\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)^8+3\left(\left(1+\frac{1}{2}\right)^9-1\right)
\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{9}{2} \cdot\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^8+3\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9-3=6 \cdot\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^9-3$
Hence, $\alpha=6, \beta=3$
Thus $(\alpha+\beta)^2=81$

solution:
Given,

x-6x32n,n≤15

Tr+1=Crnx12n-r-6x-32r

⇒Tr+1=-6r·Crn·xn-r2·x-3r2

⇒Tr+1=-6r·Crn·xn-4r2

Now we have to take, n=4r for constant term so n is divisible by 4.

So, α=Cn4n-6n4

And sum of all coefficients will be 1-6132n=-5n

Also given, -5n-Cn4n-6n4=649

Now by hit and trial we get, n=4

Hence, α=C14-644=-24

Now for coefficient of x-4

n-4r2=-4

⇒n=4r-8⇒r=3

So, λα=-63·C34

⇒λ-24=-63·C34

⇒λ=36

subject: Mathematics
options: Statement $-1$ is false, Statement $-2$ is true
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true, Statement $-2$ is a correct explanation for Statement $-1$
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is not a correct explanation for Statement $-1$.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is false.
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sum_{r=0}^n(r+1){ }^n C_r=\sum_{r=0}^n r{ }^n C_r+{ }^n C_r \\
& =\sum_{r=0}^n r \frac{n}{r}{ }^{n-1} C_{r-1}+\sum_{r=0}^n{ }^n C_r=n 2^{n-1}+2^n \\
& =2^{n-1}(n+2)
\end{aligned}
$$
Statement $-1$ is true
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sum(r+1)^n C_r x^r=\sum r{ }^n C_r x^r+\sum{ }^n C_r x^r \\
& =n \sum_{r=0}^n{ }^{n-1} C_{r-1} x^r+\sum_{r=0}^n{ }^n C_r x^r=n x(1+x)^{n-1}+(1+x)^n
\end{aligned}
$$
Substituting $x=1$
$$
\sum(r+1)^n C_r=n 2^{n-1}+2^n
$$
Hence Statement $-2$ is also true and is a correct explanation of Statement $-1$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\left(1+x+x^2\right)^{10}=a_0+a_1 x+a_2 x^2+\ldots .+a_{20} x^{20}$
$\therefore 3^{10}=\mathrm{a}_0+\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_2+\ldots .+\mathrm{a}_{20}$   ...(i)
$1=a_0-a_1+a_2 \ldots . .+a_{20}$   ...(ii)
$\text { (i) - (ii) } \Rightarrow a_1+a_3+\ldots .+a_{19}=\frac{3^{10}-1}{2}=29524$
$\begin{aligned}
\text { Also }\{ & 1+\mathrm{x}(1+\mathrm{x})\}^{10}=1 \\
& +{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{x}(1+\mathrm{x})+{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{x}^2(1+\mathrm{x})^2+\ldots .
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \mathrm{a}_2={ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_2=55$
$\therefore \frac{\left(\mathrm{a}_1+\mathrm{a}_3+\ldots+\mathrm{a}_{19}\right)-11 \mathrm{a}_2}{121}=239$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $1 + x = r$
$\therefore S = 1 \cdot r + 2 \cdot r^2 + 3 \cdot r^3 + \ldots + 100r^{100}$ ......(1) (AGP)
$rS = 1 \cdot r^2 + 2 \cdot r^3 + \ldots + 99r^{100} + 100r^{101}$ ......(2)
(1) $-$ (2) gives
$S = -\dfrac{(1+x)^{101}}{x^2} + \dfrac{1}{x^2} + \dfrac{100(1+x)^{101}}{x}$
$\therefore$ coefficient $x^{48}$ in S
$= -$ coefficient of $x^{48}$ in $\dfrac{(1+x)^{101}}{x^2} + 100 \cdot$ Coefficient of $x^{48}$ in $\dfrac{(1+x)^{101}}{x}$
$= 100 \cdot {}^{101}C_{49} - {}^{101}C_{50}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The general term in the expansion of $\left(\dfrac{1}{x^3} - x^4\right)^n$ is given by:

$T_{r+1} = ^{n}C_{r} \left(x^{-3}\right)^{n-r} \left(-x^4\right)^r = (-1)^r \cdot ^{n}C_{r} \cdot x^{7r - 3n}$

For the coefficient of $x^7$, we set the exponent to $7$:

$7r_1 - 3n = 7 \Rightarrow r_1 = \dfrac{3n + 7}{7}$

For the coefficient of $x^{14}$, we set the exponent to $14$:

$7r_2 - 3n = 14 \Rightarrow r_2 = \dfrac{3n + 14}{7}$

Notice that $r_2 = r_1 + 1$.

The sum of the coefficients of $x^7$ and $x^{14}$ is zero:

$(-1)^{r_1} \cdot ^{n}C_{r_1} + (-1)^{r_2} \cdot ^{n}C_{r_2} = 0$

$(-1)^{r_1} \cdot ^{n}C_{r_1} + (-1)^{r_1 + 1} \cdot ^{n}C_{r_1 + 1} = 0$

$(-1)^{r_1} \left( ^{n}C_{r_1} - ^{n}C_{r_1 + 1} \right) = 0$

$^{n}C_{r_1} = ^{n}C_{r_1 + 1}$

Since $r_1 \neq r_1 + 1$, we must use the property $^{n}C_{x} = ^{n}C_{y} \Rightarrow x + y = n$:

$r_1 + (r_1 + 1) = n$

$2r_1 + 1 = n$

Substituting $r_1 = \dfrac{3n + 7}{7}$:

$2\left(\dfrac{3n + 7}{7}\right) + 1 = n$

$\dfrac{6n + 14}{7} + 1 = n$

$6n + 14 + 7 = 7n$

$n = 21$

Answer: $21$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The general term of multinomial expansion is
$\frac{6!}{\alpha!\beta!\gamma!}(1)^\alpha\left(2^{\frac{1}{3}}\right)^\beta\left(3^{\frac{1}{2}}\right)^\gamma$

For terms to be rational $3 \mid \beta$ and $2 \mid \gamma$
$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}
\beta & \gamma & \alpha & \text { Term } \\
\hline 0 & 0 & 6 & 1 \\
0 & 2 & 4 & 15.3=45 \\
0 & 4 & 2 & 15.3^2=135 \\
0 & 6 & 0 & 1.3^3=27 \\
3 & 0 & 3 & 20.2=40 \\
3 & 2 & 1 & 60.2 .3=360 \\
6 & 0 & 0 & 1.4=4
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow$ Sum of rational terms
$=1+45+135+27+40+360+4=612$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & (1+x)^{n+4} \\ & { }^{n+4} C_4,{ }^{n+4} C_5,{ }^{n+4} C_6, \rightarrow \text { A.P. } \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \times { }^{n+4} C_5={ }^{n+4} C_4+{ }^{n+4} C_6 \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \times{ }^{n+4} C_5=\left({ }^{n+4} C_4+{ }^{n+4} C_5\right)+\left({ }^{n+4} C_5+{ }^{n+4} C_6\right) \\ & \Rightarrow 4 \times{ }^{n+4} C_5={ }^{n+5} C_5+{ }^{n+5} C_6\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow 4 \times \frac{(\mathrm{n}+4)!}{5!\cdot(\mathrm{n}-1)!}=\frac{(\mathrm{n}+6)!}{6!\cdot \mathrm{n}!} \\ & \Rightarrow 4=\frac{(\mathrm{n}+6)(\mathrm{n}+5)}{6 \mathrm{n}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^2+11 \mathrm{n}+30=24 \mathrm{n} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}^2-13 \mathrm{n}+30=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3,10(\text { rejected }) \\ & \because \mathrm{n} \neq 10\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Largest binomial coefficient in expansion of
$\begin{aligned}
& (1+\mathrm{x})^7 \\
& (\because \mathrm{n}+4=7)
\end{aligned}$
is coeff. of middle term
$\Rightarrow{ }^7 \mathrm{C}_4={ }^7 \mathrm{C}_3=35$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=1+\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})}{1 !}+\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^2}{2 !}+\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^3}{3 !}+\ldots . . \\
& \frac{\mathrm{e}^{(1+\mathrm{x})}}{1+\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1}{1+\mathrm{x}}+1+\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})}{2 !}+\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^2}{3 !}+\frac{(1+\mathrm{x})^2}{4 !} \\
& \text { coef } \mathrm{x}^2 \text { in RHS : } 1+\frac{{ }^2 \mathrm{C}_2}{3}+\frac{{ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2}{4}+\ldots=\mathrm{a}
\end{aligned}$
coeff. $x^2$ in L.H.S.
$e\left(1+x+\frac{x^2}{2 !}\right) \ldots\left(1-x+\frac{x^2}{2 !} \ldots \ldots\right)$
is $\mathrm{e}-\mathrm{e}+\frac{\mathrm{e}}{2 !}=\mathrm{a}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{b}=1+\frac{2}{1 !}+\frac{2^2}{2 !}+\frac{2^3}{3 !}+\ldots \ldots=\mathrm{e}^2 \\
& \frac{2 \mathrm{~b}}{\mathrm{a}^2}=8
\end{aligned}$

solution:

ar=C10-r10=Cr10

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The general term in the expansion of $\left(2x^2 + \dfrac{1}{x}\right)^{10}$ is given by:

$T_{r+1} = ^{10}C_{r} (2x^2)^{10-r} \left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)^r$

$T_{r+1} = ^{10}C_{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-2r} x^{-r}$

$T_{r+1} = ^{10}C_{r} 2^{10-r} x^{20-3r}$

For the coefficient of $x^2$, we equate the power of $x$ to $2$:

$20 - 3r = 2$

$3r = 18 \Rightarrow r = 6$

Substituting $r = 6$ in the coefficient part, we get:

Coefficient $= ^{10}C_{6} 2^{10-6}$

$= ^{10}C_{4} 2^4$

$= \dfrac{10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1} \times 16$

$= 210 \times 16$

$= 3360$

Answer: $3360$
